retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthyroidism in Pregnancy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Differentiate between normal physiological changes in thyroid function during pregnancy and pathological hyperthyroidism. Apply evidence-based management strategies to balance maternal symptom control with fetal safety, incorporating current clinical guidelines for pharmacologic and nonpharmacologic interventions. Implement evidence-based options for the management of hyperthyroidism in pregnant patients to ensure maternal and fetal safety. Collaborate with an interprofessional healthcare team to optimize the management of hyperthyroidism in pregnancy and improve maternal and fetal outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy -- Treatment / Management -- Hyperthyroidism
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment goals for hyperthyroidism during pregnancy are maintaining mild maternal hyperthyroidism while avoiding fetal hypothyroidism. [37] To maintain mild maternal hyperthyroidism, maternal free T4/total T4 should be maintained at the upper limit of normal of the reference range using the lowest effective dose of antithyroid drugs. If the TSH and total T4 levels are within normal limits, the fetus is likely exposed to excessive anti-thyroid drugs. The treatment for hyperthyroidism during pregnancy is indicated based on etiology as well as the severity of hyperthyroidism.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy -- Treatment / Management -- Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: If Graves disease has been previously treated outside of pregnancy with thyroidectomy or ablative therapy, there may be persistent thyroid receptor antibodies. These antibodies are immunoglobulin G proteins that can cross the placenta and cause fetal hyperthyroidism. [2] [13] [33] In this special scenario, treatment with a block-and-replace strategy may be warranted. This entails treating fetal hyperthyroidism with antithyroid drug therapy while simultaneously maintaining maternal euthyroid status via levothyroxine, which does not cross the placenta as easily as thioamides. [33] Further, radioactive iodine ablation is contraindicated during pregnancy. Subclinical hyperthyroidism, as well as gestational thyrotoxicosis, do not require treatment during pregnancy, and rather, observation is recommended with periodic monitoring of thyroid function tests every 4 to 6 weeks.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyrotoxicosis -- Treatment / Management -- Radioiodine Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Gestational transient thyrotoxicosis typically resolves naturally as hCG levels decline around 10 to 12 weeks of gestation and is not associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes. Treatment focuses on supportive care for nausea, vomiting, and electrolyte imbalances, particularly in cases of hyperemesis gravidarum, without the need for antithyroid drugs. [47] Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Hyperthyroidism in Pregnancy, " for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy -- Treatment / Management -- Antithyroid Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: Thionamides can cross the placental barrier but should not cause fetal hypothyroidism when appropriately dosed. [26] The lowest dose of antithyroid medication should be used to achieve this, with a TSH target slightly lower than the reference range and a maternal-free T4 at the high end of normal. As noted above, if the TSH becomes normal, it likely means that the fetus is receiving too much anti-thyroid drug. [2] [3] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthyroidism in Pregnancy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Surgery is optimally performed outside of pregnancy. In women who do not attain adequate control of hyperthyroidism with high doses of ATDs, who have an allergy to ATDs, or who are poorly compliant with therapy, surgery can be considered. Surgery is also an option for patients who have a large goiter causing compression issues. Total or subtotal thyroidectomy can be performed in pregnancy, preferably in the second trimester, when the risk of fetal loss and complications is lowest. [2] [3] [5] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Thyroiditis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A prospective study that evaluated 605 asymptomatic pregnant women and women in the postpartum period showed that none of the cases with thyrotoxicosis and 40% of cases with hypothyroid state needed treatment. If treatment is necessitated, it could usually be tapered over one year. Several studies have shown that long-term treatment would be required in up to 20% of cases with postpartum thyroiditis. There have not been any prospective studies to evaluate when and how to treat postpartum thyroiditis. Management of the thyrotoxic state is guided by its transitory nature. Anti-thyroid medications (methimazole and propylthiouracil) have been ineffective in the treatment of the thyrotoxic state of postpartum thyroiditis as it is destructive thyroiditis in which there is no increase in the synthesis of thyroid hormones. Symptoms are usually mild. In a few cases with clinically significant symptoms, a low dose of propranolol may be helpful. The thyrotoxic state of postpartum thyroiditis should be differentiated from Graves disease. When thyrotoxic state of postpartum thyroiditis resolved, the serum TSH level should be measured in about four to eight weeks (or in case of development of new symptoms) in order to screen for the hypothyroid state. In cases who develop significant symptoms, and currently lactating or cases who want to get pregnant, treatment should be initiated. Treatment with levothyroxine (LT4) 4 should be considered in the hypothyroid state of postpartum thyroiditis if the patient has mild symptoms, or advice if the patient wants to get pregnant again. If treatment is delayed, thyroid function must be evaluated every four to eight weeks until the patient gets euthyroid. In addition, women must be counseled to have contraception. The duration that the patient should continue to take LT4 has not been evaluated. Based on the guidelines, the euthyroid state should be maintained in cases who are pregnant or wants to get pregnant. To assess if the hypothyroid state of postpartum thyroiditis is permanent or transient, LT4 doses can be tapered starting 12 months after delivery. The dose should be tapered gradually, and serum TSH level must be monitored every six to eight weeks. [8]

retrieved_docs.content: Graves disease is the most common cause of thyrotoxicosis during pregnancy. Although the autoantibodies do not attack the fetal thyroid, excess maternal thyroid can cause fetal/neonate central hypothyroidism by suppressing the pituitary release of TSH. retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthyroidism in Pregnancy -- Treatment / Management -- Fetal Surveillance
retrieved_docs.content: TRAb should be remeasured between 18 and 22 weeks and 30 and 34 weeks to evaluate the risk of fetal and neonatal hyperthyroidism, respectively. [2] [5] While there is no consensus among professional societies, further monitoring may be indicated if TRAb levels exceed 3 times the upper limit of normal or if there is a history of a newborn affected by a thyroid disorder. [3] Further monitoring may include serial growth ultrasounds, amniotic fluid measurements, evaluation of the fetal heart rate, and fetal thyroid ultrasound to check for goiter. [3] [5] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthyroidism in Pregnancy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If Graves' disease has been previously treated outside of pregnancy with thyroidectomy or ablative therapy, there may be TRAbs that persist. These antibodies are immunoglobulin G proteins and can cross the placenta, potentially causing fetal hyperthyroidism. [1] [9] [12] In this special scenario, treatment with a block-and-replace strategy may be warranted. This approach entails treating the fetal hyperthyroidism with ATD therapy while simultaneously maintaining maternal euthyroid status via levothyroxine, which does not cross the placenta as easily as ATDs. [5] [9] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Differentiate between normal pregnancy-related thyroid adaptations and pathological thyroid dysfunction. Screen pregnant individuals effectively for thyroid disease using appropriate laboratory and clinical assessments. Implement evidence-based treatment plans for hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism tailored to gestational age and patient needs. Collaborate among an interprofessional healthcare team to effectively detect thyroid disease in pregnancy and subsequent monitoring. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthyroidism in Pregnancy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: When treatment is initiated, dose-adjusted, or transitioned between drugs, thyroid function tests should be obtained to confirm a euthyroid state; testing can be performed every 2 to 4 weeks as indicated to ensure the maintenance of euthyroid status. [3] [11] [13] PTU and methimazole are both effective ATDs, but require different doses due to different pharmacokinetics. When switching, a 1:20 ratio of methimazole/PTU can be used as the initial conversion factor. [5] PTU is given 100 to 300 mg daily, divided into 3 doses due to a shorter half-life than methimazole, which is dosed 5 to 15 mg once daily. [9] Thus, switching between the drugs may result in a period of hyperthyroidism until the dose can be appropriately adjusted. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthyroidism in Pregnancy -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperthyroidism during pregnancy is rare but marked by elevated T4 and T3 levels with suppressed TSH, and prompt diagnosis is critical to prevent maternal and fetal complications. While achieving euthyroid status before conception is ideal, unplanned pregnancies make early detection essential. Early recognition and appropriate management can significantly improve outcomes for both mother and baby.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthyroidism in Pregnancy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Radioiodine ablation (RAI) is a procedure that can be used to destroy active thyroid tissue. However, RAI is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy due to the ability of radioiodine to cross the placenta and subsequently ablate the fetal thyroid, leading to congenital hypothyroidism. [3] [5] [8] [13] Before fetal thyroid development, RAI carries a risk for spontaneous abortion or fetal malformations. Women who elect to undergo RAI outside of pregnancy are advised to avoid conception for at least 6 months. [3] [5] [8] [13] This recommendation ensures clearance of radioiodine and allows adequate timing to achieve a stable euthyroid state with levothyroxine. [13] Further, beta-blockers such as propranolol can be used in pregnancy for symptomatic control until a euthyroid state is maintained. [1] [2] [5] Once the euthyroid status is stable, beta-blockers should be discontinued due to the risk of intrauterine growth restriction, fetal bradycardia, and neonatal hypoglycemia with continued use. [2] [3] [9] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthyroidism in Pregnancy -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Subclinical hyperthyroidism is typically not associated with adverse effects and does not require treatment. [2] [5] [10] [12] Treatment can lead to fetal hypothyroidism and adverse outcomes. [13] Using the correct trimester-specific reference ranges and interpreting test results appropriately helps avoid unnecessary treatment while also preventing adverse outcomes associated with undertreatment. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy is a broad category that includes several specific maternal health issues. It has the child condition قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (light vomiting of pregnancy) and is classified as encompassing الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before delivery), الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل (German measles during pregnancy), and خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل (thyroid dysfunction in the gestational period). These various complications highlight the diverse risks that can affect pregnant individuals and underscore the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and appropriate management to protect both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2281
retrieved_docs.content: Mild hyperemesis gravidarum, thyroid dysfunction during pregnancy, and gestational herpes are all specific maternal complications that fall under the broader, uncategorized group of pregnancy complications affecting the mother. Each of these conditions represents a distinct health issue that can arise during gestation and requires appropriate clinical attention despite not being classified within more specific subcategories of maternal pregnancy disorders. Recognizing them as part of the general category of unclassified maternal pregnancy complications helps clinicians consider a wider range of potential problems when evaluating and managing pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2661
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy represent a broad category of maternal health issues that include specific conditions such as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before birth), خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل (thyroid dysfunction during pregnancy), and فقر الدم في أثناء الحمل (anemia during pregnancy). Recognizing these sub‑conditions under the umbrella of other complications of pregnancy is important for comprehensive prenatal assessment, as each can affect fetal development and maternal outcomes. Effective screening and management of drug dependence, thyroid abnormalities, and anemia are essential components of obstetric care to reduce risks to both mother and child.

retrieved_docs.content: assessment, early identification of abnormal presentations, cervical pathology, uterine functional disturbances, and multiple‑gestation status, together with avoidance of teratogenic drugs, are essential for individualized management strategies that optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes during labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: constitute major complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. Recognizing the placement of each specific condition within its broader category supports comprehensive assessment, risk stratification, and coordinated management of pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1021
retrieved_docs.content: organizes general obstetric procedure categories (إجراء توليدي, Antepartum Procedures, Obstetrics incision) and specific clinical actions—ranging from diagnostic sampling and membrane management to fetal stimulation, reduction of multiple gestations, and surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancies—providing a comprehensive framework for coordinating obstetric care across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and postpartum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: each condition as a potential adverse event, enabling systematic risk assessment, targeted monitoring, operative decision‑making, and coordinated maternal‑fetal and newborn care aimed at reducing morbidity and improving outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2759
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is regarded as an allowable therapeutic option in several specific clinical contexts. It is permitted for patients presenting with وضعية المَجيء المقعدي, for individuals who have thyroid disorders, and in situations of تهديد بالإجهاض (threatened abortion). This indicates that radiotherapy is not contraindicated in these particular conditions and may be considered appropriate when clinical judgment deems it necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2809
retrieved_docs.content: Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is clinically associated with several obstetric conditions, including gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل), threatened miscarriage (تهديد بالإجهاض), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد). These associations highlight the importance of evaluating and managing metabolic, hematologic, and early‑pregnancy complications when counseling patients about elective pregnancy termination, ensuring comprehensive risk assessment and optimal maternal health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1053
retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is a broad diagnostic category that includes COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY. Within this category, a specific manifestation is Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester, which is further classified as Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage. Recognizing that spotting in an unspecified trimester represents an early‑trimester hemorrhagic event and is considered a pregnancy‑related complication helps clinicians organize maternal conditions that affect pregnancy, delivery, or the puerperium and guides appropriate assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2790
retrieved_docs.content: إنهاء الحمل - اختياري, which denotes an elective termination of pregnancy, is clinically associated with تهديد بالإجهاض, a condition described as threatened abortion. This clinical association indicates that the two obstetric entities share overlapping concerns, and that the presence of a threatened abortion may influence considerations regarding elective pregnancy termination in patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are clinically associated with إنهاء الحمل – اختياري (optional termination of pregnancy) and also have a relationship with اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder). This indicates that uterine disorders are considered among the other complications of pregnancy and that optional termination of pregnancy can be clinically linked to these pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Poor reproductive history and elective termination of pregnancy are clinically associated with preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This relationship indicates that women who have experienced a suboptimal reproductive history or who have undergone an elective termination of pregnancy may be at increased risk for developing preterm labor. Recognizing these associations can help clinicians identify patients who require closer monitoring and targeted interventions to prevent or manage preterm labor, thereby improving maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2304
retrieved_docs.content: Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage is classified as spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester.
